coredata.dc:title: The effect of liquid channels and cold plates on the performance of hybrid thermal management strategies for cylindrical Li-ion cells
coredata.prism:publicationName: e-Prime - Advances in Electrical Engineering, Electronics and Energy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Shahid, Seham
Agelin-Chaab, Martin
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, phase change material (PCM) based hybrid thermal management strategies are proposed for cylindrical Lithium-ion battery modules. Experimental and numerical studies are conducted to analyze the proposed strategies at a high discharge rate of 7 C. Three different types of thermal management configurations are developed. In all the configurations primary coolant is the PCM and secondary coolants are liquid and air. In one of the configuration vertical liquid coolant channels are placed between the PCM and airflow at the top of the cells. Whereas, in the two configurations different designs of cold plates are utilized in place of liquid channels. The experimental studies are conducted to obtain the temperature and heat flux profiles of the battery module. Moreover, a numerical model is developed and validated using the experimental data obtained. Using the numerical model, the thermal performance of the battery module was obtained for the proposed strategies. Based on the results, the maximum temperature of the battery module was limited to 29.1 °C and a high temperature uniformity was achieved by the end of the discharge cycle with the temperature difference limited to 1.2 °C. Additionally, the proposed hybrid thermal management strategy was capable of maintaining the required optimal thermal operating conditions without changing the phase of the PCM. Furthermore, it eliminates the requirement of a pump and reservoir since there is no flow of liquid within the battery module. This reduces the energy required for the operation of the thermal management system, thereby increasing the available energy for propulsion. Additionally, the developed strategy also increases the safety of the battery modules as it is capable of maintaining the required thermal environment in high discharge rates up to 7 C.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Phase change material
Hybrid cooling
Liquid channels
Battery thermal management
Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD)
Li-ion cylindrical cells

Experiment 2 – heat flux measurement for numerical boundary condition
Mesh independence and time independence studies
Heat flux profile at different discharge rates
Overview of thermal profiles of the proposed strategies
Effects of secondary liquid and air cooling

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Isometric view, (b) top cross-section view, (c) A-A section view, and (d) B-B section view of Strategy 1.
(a) Isometric view and (b) top cross-section view of Strategy 2; (c) isometric view and (d) top cross-section view of Strategy 3.
Experiment 1 - (a) top view picture and (b) schematic of the experimental setup.
Experiment 2 - (a) top view picture and (b) schematic of the experimental setup.
(a) Isometric view and (b)cross-section view at the center of the battery module mesh.

(a) Velocity profiles at the center of the air duct for the different mesh sizes; (b) Comparison of the average velocity for the different mesh sizes; (c) Temporal velocities at the center of the air duct for the different time step sizes.
Transient heat flux at different discharge rates.
Maximum and minimum temperature for (a) Strategy 1, (b) Strategy 2, and (c) Strategy 3.
Temperature difference between the maximum and minimum temperature for Strategies 1, 2, and 3.
Temperature contours at the end of the discharge cycle for (a) Strategy 1, (b) Strategy 2, and (c) Strategy 3.
Temperature contours at the center of the battery module at the end of the discharge cycle for (a) Strategy 1, (b) Strategy 2, and (c) Strategy 3.
Temperature contours of (a) liquid channel in Strategy 1, and (b) cold plates for Strategy 2, and (c) Strategy 3.
Temperature contours of air contact surfaces in (a) Strategy 1, (b) Strategy 2, and (c) Strategy 3.
Physical properties of materials used for Experiment 1.
Physical properties of materials used for numerical simulations of Strategies 1, 2, and 3.

head.ce:title: The effect of liquid channels and cold plates on the performance of hybrid thermal management strategies for cylindrical Li-ion cells

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Engineering and Applied Science, Ontario Tech University, 2000 Simcoe Street North, Oshawa, Ontario, L1H 7K4, Canada

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Engineering and Applied Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Engineering and Applied Science, Ontario Tech University, 2000 Simcoe Street North, Oshawa, Ontario, L1H 7K4, Canada

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, phase change material (PCM) based hybrid thermal management strategies are proposed for cylindrical Lithium-ion battery modules. Experimental and numerical studies are conducted to analyze the proposed strategies at a high discharge rate of 7 C. Three different types of thermal management configurations are developed. In all the configurations primary coolant is the PCM and secondary coolants are liquid and air. In one of the configuration vertical liquid coolant channels are placed between the PCM and airflow at the top of the cells. Whereas, in the two configurations different designs of cold plates are utilized in place of liquid channels. The experimental studies are conducted to obtain the temperature and heat flux profiles of the battery module. Moreover, a numerical model is developed and validated using the experimental data obtained. Using the numerical model, the thermal performance of the battery module was obtained for the proposed strategies. Based on the results, the maximum temperature of the battery module was limited to 29.1 °C and a high temperature uniformity was achieved by the end of the discharge cycle with the temperature difference limited to 1.2 °C. Additionally, the proposed hybrid thermal management strategy was capable of maintaining the required optimal thermal operating conditions without changing the phase of the PCM. Furthermore, it eliminates the requirement of a pump and reservoir since there is no flow of liquid within the battery module. This reduces the energy required for the operation of the thermal management system, thereby increasing the available energy for propulsion. Additionally, the developed strategy also increases the safety of the battery modules as it is capable of maintaining the required thermal environment in high discharge rates up to 7 C.

ce:def-description.ce:para: user defined function for continuity equation

ce:section.ce:para: The reduction of greenhouse gas emissions is one of the major challenges the transportation industry is facing. Currently, it emits 29% of the total emissions [1]. One of the promising alternatives is the introduction of electrified powertrains and Lithium-ion (Li-ion) battery packs are crucial for these electric vehicles (EV). The advantage of Li-ion cells lies in their low rate of self-discharge, high energy and power density, and extended lifecycle [2–4]. However, one of the challenges the industry faces in the implementation of Li-ion cells is the optimal thermal requirement for effective and safe operation [5]. These optimal thermal conditions are sustained through an efficient thermal management system (TMS).

Currently, there are three main primary coolants that are used within the TMS. These consist of air, liquid, and phase change material (PCM). The most cost-effective TMS is based on air cooling. However, the cooling capabilities are limited since air has a low thermal conductivity and heat capacity [6].

In comparison to air, liquids have improved cooling capabilities due to higher thermal conductivity and heat capacity [7]. For liquid cooling, there are two categories of fluids that are used. The first category is the electrically insulating fluids. Since these fluids do not allow the charge to pass through them, therefore, they are used in direct contact with the Li-ion cells to extract the heat from the surface of the cells [8–10]. However, this category of fluids usually has high viscosities, which increase the pumping power associated with the flow of the fluid throughout the entire battery pack. In addition, to increase power requirements, battery packs that contain these fluids have to be properly insulated to avoid fluid leakage, as it could result in adverse and unwanted effects on the battery pack [11]. The second category of fluids is the electrically non-insulating fluids. These fluids allow the current to flow through them and cannot be placed in direct contact with the surface of the cells. A cold plate is used for these fluids and is placed in direct contact with the cells. It contains liquid channels through which the fluid flows and cools the cold plate, and extracts heat from the cells. In comparison to insulating fluids, these fluids have lower viscosities and, as a result, require less pumping power for the flow of the fluid through the battery pack. However, the application of the cold plates is limited to prismatic and pouch cells because the design complexity and manufacturing difficulty are greater for cylindrical cells [12–14].

Serpentine liquid channels are utilized in battery packs with cylindrical cells to incorporate electrically non-insulating fluids and act as an alternate solution to cold plates. These channels run through the entire row of the cylindrical cells and are curved in a zig-zag manner to encapsulate the maximum amount of surface area of the cells. Through the use of these channels, a temperature non-uniformity occurs with each cell. The surface of the cell in direct contact with the channel has a lower temperature compared to the surface of the cell that is not in contact with the channel. Zhao et al. [15] investigated various configurations of the serpentine liquid channels. As expected, the highest temperature uniformity was achieved by increasing the direct contact surface area of the channel in the streamwise direction. The second highest temperature uniformity was achieved by the design in which multiple serpentine channels were used, which shortened the flow path for each channel. Rao et al. [16] attempted to develop a novel alternate solution by incorporating variable-length aluminum blocks. These blocks were placed around the surface of the cylindrical cells, and the length was changed in the streamwise direction to increase temperature uniformity. Liquid channels were placed within these aluminum blocks and effectively removed the heat from the cells and the aluminum blocks. However, the pumping power required for fluid flow through the entire battery pack can increase significantly, which reduces the energy available for the propulsion of the EV [17].

PCMs are also used as a primary cooling medium to extract heat from the cells using latent heat during the phase change process. Initially, its use was suggested by Al-Hallaj and Selman [18]. The study conducted by Javani et al. [19] concluded that a high level of temperature uniformity was achieved over the battery submodules. Moreover, Jiang et al. [20] applied a composite PCM to the battery pack to achieve the maximum temperature range of 41 – 44 °C at 5 C cell discharge rate. The C-rate is defined as the charge / discharge current divided by nominally rated battery capacity. This composite PCM was developed by combining expanded graphite (EG) and paraffin wax to increase thermal conductivity. In comparison, through forced air cooling of 1 m/s, the maximum temperature of 72 °C was obtained for the same battery pack. The temperature uniformity was also investigated, and with the use of composite PCM, a 1 – 2 °C temperature uniformity was achieved. He et al. [21] also conducted an investigation with the EG/paraffin composite PCM. Temperature uniformity of 2.82 °C was achieved, and 7% of EG was considered the optimum proportion of EG within the composite PCM. Furthermore, a novel PCM composition was developed and investigated by Hussain et al. [22]. For the composite PCM, the nickel foam was coated with saturated paraffin and graphene. The results showed that the thermal conductivity of the composite PCM increased 23 folds when compared to pure paraffin. Ye et al. [23] designed and developed a novel composite PCM (CPCM). This CPCM consists of dual phase change temperature regions. At regular ambient temperatures, the lower phase change temperature region is utilized and maintains the module temperature between 25.9 - 34.9 °C. Whereas at higher ambient temperatures, the higher phase change temperature region is utilized, and the temperature of the module is maintained below 49.2 °C. The authors also added EG to the CPCM to increase the thermal conductivity to 1.98 W/m.K. Furthermore, Wu et al. [24] developed a novel flexible CPCM to increase the contact surface area between the CPCM and the cells and minimize the thermal contact resistance. The developed flexible CPCM shows better flexibility between the temperature range of −5 to 20 °C. It also has an increased thermal conductivity of 1.64 W/m.K. The results exhibited 4 °C lower maximum temperature for cylindrical cells when compared to rigid CPCM. Yousefi et al. [25] studied the impact of nanoparticles in the PCM on the performance of the TMS. It was found that by adding the CuO nanoparticles the temperature of the cells reduced and the melting time of the PCM increased. Even though the application of PCM in TMS shows promising results, it is not widely used due to the low overall heat transfer coefficient of the PCM. Once the latent heat of the PCM is completely utilized and the PCM has completely melted, then the PCM acts as a thermal insulator, and the heat extraction process is terminated. Therefore, a secondary cooling system, either air cooling or liquid cooling, is required to extract the heat away from the PCM itself.

In order to overcome the drawbacks presented by the individual air, liquid, and PCM based TMS, researchers are introducing and developing hybrid TMS, which combines two or three cooling mediums to achieve the effective thermal performance of the TMS. One of the most intuitive hybrid cooling concepts is to introduce mist in the airflow. A study by Saw et al. [4] on mist cooling concluded that in order to maintain the cell surface temperature below 40 °C, 5 g/s mass flowrate and 3% mist are required. In addition to this, evaporative and convective cooling was investigated by Wei and Agelin-Chaab [6,26]. Liquid channel strips were located in-between the cells, and water flowed through the channel strips using capillary action. This was used to recycle the axial airflow flowing over the cells. Through the recycling process, the air managed to regain a portion of its heat extracting capacity which resulted in a 56% increment in temperature uniformity and 20% in the cooling of the cells. Wang et al. [27] investigated a hybrid TMS in which cold plates were located at the bottom of the cylindrical cells, and the air was circulated such that it was recycled using the cold plate. Multiple configurations of fan placement were investigated, and a fully developed flow field was achieved when the fan was placed under the cold plates. Through this strategy, the temperature uniformity improved by 2.42 times and the maximum temperature was reduced by 3.45 °C when compared to traditional individual TMS. Additionally, a hybrid TMS combining air and composite PCM was developed and optimized by Ling et al. [28]. The results showed that the maximum temperature was maintained below 37 °C and temperature uniformity below 3 °C. Moreover, through the optimization study, the PCM mass was reduced by 94.1% and the volume of the PCM by 55.6%. In their previous study [29] the authors developed a hybrid concept in which liquid jackets were attached to each of the cylindrical cells, and the liquid constrained within the jackets was in direct contact with the surface of the cells. Additionally, jet inlets and vortex generators developed in earlier studies [30–33] were added to the battery pack to improve mixing and turbulence within the airflow. The results concluded that temperature uniformity was maintained within 1.26 °C. Khaboshan et al. [34] utilized three passive methods which include PCM, metal foam and fins to develop a hybrid TMS. The results showed that a combination of all three methods reduced the maximum temperature by 3 K and delayed the melting of the PCM by 470 s.

This paper introduces three novel hybrid thermal management strategies for cylindrical Li-ion battery modules. In all the strategies the primary coolant is the PCM and secondary coolants are liquid and air. In one of the strategies vertical liquid coolant channels are placed between the PCM and airflow at the top of the cells. Whereas, in the two strategies different designs of cold plates are utilized in place of liquid channels. The primary objective is to minimize the increase in temperature and achieve a high level of temperature uniformity while utilizing the least amount of PCM, since a low utilization will result in an increased capability of heat transfer. The secondary objective is to either utilize the least amount of pumping power or eliminate the pumping power for the flow of the fluid. Therefore, the fluid within the liquid channels and cold plates is stationary as opposed to continuous flowing fluid in liquid based TMS in the open literature. Finally, the tertiary objective is to minimize the airflow velocity through the use of laminar flow to achieve the primary objective.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Description of proposed hybrid TMS strategies
ce:section.ce:para: This study proposes three different types of hybrid TMS strategies. In Strategy 1, a single primary coolant and dual secondary coolants are utilized. The primary coolant is the PCM, and the secondary coolants are liquid and air. At the core, there are cylindrical Li-ion cells which are in direct contact with the PCM. In between the PCM, there are liquid channels that run vertically. The fluid in these channels is stationary. An air duct is added to the top of the cells to allow airflow, and the opening of the liquid channel is in the air duct. The working principle behind this is divided into three stages. In the initial stage, the heat from the surface of the cell will be extracted using the PCM through direct contact. In the intermediary stage, the heat from the PCM will be extracted using stationary fluid within the liquid channels. Through added heat from the PCM into the fluid, the natural evaporation process will accelerate, and the evaporated vapors will move into the air duct in the final stage. These evaporated vapours will then be removed from the system and into the environment through forced airflow within the air duct. Once the fluid in the liquid channel is consumed through natural evaporation and the fluid level falls, then a small pump and reservoir will be required to top up the fluid. Strategy 1 is shown in Fig. 1 using computer aided design (CAD) models.

Strategy 2 was developed by modifying Strategy 1 and is shown in Fig. 2 (a) and (b). The circular liquid channel in between the PCM was changed to the rectangular cold plate, which is completely filled with fluid and protrudes in the air duct. This cold plate is sealed and closed from all sides so that there is no leakage of fluid. Two of these cold plates are placed in between each cell row in the longitudinal direction such that the larger surface area of the protrusion in the air duct is in line with airflow. The working principle behind this strategy is similar to Strategy 1, with a few key differences. The initial stage is the same as the PCM will extract heat from the surface of the cells. The intermediary stage is modified by removing the evaporation factor. In this stage, the fluid within the cold plates will extract heat from the PCM. In the final stage, rather than removing fluid vapors from the system, the air will pass over the protruded cold plate and will be used to cool the fluid within it. For comparison purposes, the volume of fluid within the liquid channels in Strategy 1 and cold plates in Strategy 2 was kept the same. Since the fluid in this strategy is sealed, therefore, no pumping power is required to top up the fluid, which eliminates the requirement for a fluid pump and reservoir.

Strategy 2 was further modified to develop Strategy 3 and is shown in Fig. 2(c) and (d). The geometry of the cold plate was changed so that it could be placed in between each cell column as well. As shown in Fig. 2(d), the cold plates are all connected to each other, and the fluid within this is considered a single body of fluid. The protrusion of the cold plate within the air duct is, however, the same as Strategy 2, as shown in Fig. 2(c). Due to a single fluid body, the same protrusion geometry will allow the fluid in between the columns to cool as well. The working principle for this strategy is identical to Strategy 2. Since Strategy 3 is an improvement to Strategy 2 therefore, the volume of fluid was increased at the expense of PCM volume.

For this study, two different types of experiments were performed. The first experiment was performed to validate the numerical model. A battery module was developed, as shown in Fig. 1 and temperature measurements of the cells were obtained and compared with the results of the numerical model. The second experiment was performed to obtain the transient heat flux values at the surface of the cylindrical Li-ion cell. These heat flux values were then used in the validated numerical model as a boundary condition to obtain the thermal performance of the battery module. The details of the setup and procedure of each experiment are provided in the following sub-sections.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experiment 1 – temperature measurement for model validation
ce:section.ce:para: This experiment was executed to measure and obtain the temperature profile of the cells within the battery module. The setup and schematics of the experiment are shown in Fig. 3 . A battery module was developed using the CAD design shown in Fig. 1. The housing of the module was manufactured using wood. Nine Samsung INR18650–25R cylindrical Li-ion cells were added to the battery module and were connected in a series arrangement. The properties of the cell are provided in Table 1 .

Since the cells used in battery packs do not have electrical insulation, therefore, to get accurate results in the temperature measurements, the electrical insulation was removed from the surface of the cell. The liquid channels were made using aluminum tubes and were filled with water after placing them in the battery module. Single T-type thermocouples with an absolute bias error of 1 °C was attached to the cell to measure the temperature of the cell. Paraffin wax was used as the PCM. The PCM was melted and then poured into the battery module in liquid form. Once poured, it was then allowed to solidify to capture the form of the cavity in the battery module. In order to force the airflow, the duct an axial fan (GDSTIME AXIAL Fan 12,038) was used, and Proster Anemometer was used to measure the airflow velocity. A Turnigy Reaktor Quadkore charger was used to charge the lithium-ion batteries. To power the charger, a direct current (DC) power supply was used. All nine cells were discharged in series using a multi-functional load discharger (TDI RBL488). The measurements from the thermocouples were captured using a National Instrument (NI) data acquisition device (DAQ) NI-9211 combined with NI cDAQ-9174 chassis. Finally, LabView software was used to capture the reading from the NI-DAQ module.

The experiment was initiated by charging all the cells within the battery module using the battery charger. The charging was done under constant current (CC) and constant voltage (CV). In this, the cell was charged at a CC of 1 A until the cut-off voltage of 4.3 V for each cell was achieved (37.8 V for all the cells in series). At this point, the cells were charged at a CV of 37.8 V until the charging current was 0 A. The battery module was then kept at a room temperature of 25 °C. The fan was then turned on, and the velocity was measured at the inlet of the battery module using a Proster anemometer. Once the readings from the thermocouples were measured to be 25 °C, then the discharge process was started. The cell was discharged at a CC discharging rate, and the temperatures from the thermocouples were recorded using the LabView software. The discharge process was terminated when the voltage of the battery module reached 22.5 V. The experiment was conducted at a discharge rate of 1 C. An uncertainty analysis was performed using the method suggested by Moffat [36]. Based on the uncertainty analysis, the average percentage uncertainty in the temperature measurement was estimated to be ± 2%. The properties of the different materials used for this experiment are shown in Table 2 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experiment 2 – heat flux measurement for numerical boundary condition
ce:section.ce:para: This experiment was conducted to obtain heat flux measurements at the surface of the cell. This heat flux measurement was then used as the boundary condition in the numerical modeling. The setup of this experiment is shown in Fig. 4 (a), and the schematics are shown in Fig. 4(b). The Samsung INR18650–25R Li-ion cell with Aluminum tabs attached to positive and negative terminals was used to measure the heat flux. The diameter of the cell is 18 mm, and the length of the cell is 65 mm. The cathode material is composed of LiNiMnCoO2, and the anode material is composed of graphite. The nominal discharge capacity is 2.5 Ah with a nominal voltage of 3.6 V [35]. The electrical insulation was removed from the surface of the cell to get accurate measurements, as the non-insulated cells are used in the battery module. To charge the Li-ion cell, a Turnigy Reaktor Quadkore charger is used. A direct current (DC) power supply (Jesverty SPS-3010 N) was used to power the Turnigy Reaktor. To discharge the batteries, a multi-functional load discharger (TDI RBL488) is used. An Omega HFS-5 sensor was used for the heat flux measurements. To measure the readings from the HFS-5 sensor, a National Instrument (NI) data acquisition device (DAQ) NI-9211 combined with NI cDAQ-9174 chassis was used. Finally, LabView software was used to capture the reading from the DAQ module.

As shown in Fig. 4(a), alligator clips were connected to the positive and negative tabs of the Li-ion cell. The cell was charged at 1 A CC current. During this stage, the voltage of the cell increased gradually. Once the voltage reached 4.2 V it was charged at CV and the charging current was reduced from 1 A to 0 A. Once the cell was completely charged, then it was kept at room temperature of 25 °C. Then the discharge process started, and the cell was discharged at 2.5 A CC discharging rate (which corresponds to 1 C discharge rate) until the voltage dropped to the cut-off voltage of 2.5 V. During the discharge process, the data was recorded using the LabView software. An uncertainty analysis was conducted using the method suggested by Moffat [36] to estimate the accuracy of the experimental results. Based on the uncertainty analysis, the average percentage uncertainty in the heat flux measurement was estimated to be ± 2%.

Numerical modeling was performed for the three proposed strategies. To conduct the numerical modeling, ANSYS Fluent, a commercial computational fluid dynamics (CFD) software, was used. The numerical modeling was achieved through the coupling of the individual flow, energy, and solidification/melting models. Since laminar flow was considered for the airflow within the duct; therefore, the flow model does not include any turbulence modeling. The governing equations applicable to the flow and energy models are shown below [37].

Continuity Equation
(1)

The solidification/melting model is used within the PCM region where phase change will occur between the defined temperatures using latent heat. The enthalpy-porosity technique is used to model the phase change process. In this technique, the interfaces where the solid melts are not explicitly tracked. Instead, a liquid fraction (β) quantity is introduced, which provides the fraction of each cell volume that is in liquid form. This liquid fraction is based on the enthalpy balance. The enthalpy of the PCM is based on the latent heat and sensible enthalpy, as shown below [37].
(5)
H = h + β L

The liquid fraction is 0 if the temperature (T) is less than the solidification temperature (Tsolidus) of the PCM. Additionally, the liquid fraction is 1 if the temperature (T) is more than the melting temperature (Tliquidus) of the PCM. The equation for the liquid fraction during the change of phase is provided below [37].
(6)
β =
T −
T
s o l i d u s
T
l i q u i d u s
−
T
s o l i d u s

Therefore, the energy equation is as follows.
(7)
∂
∂ T
(
ρ H
)
+ ∇ ·
(
ρ
v →
H
)
= ∇ ·
(
k ∇ T
)
+ S

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mesh independence and time independence studies
ce:section.ce:para: The developed mesh is shown in Fig. 5 . A combination of tetrahedral and hexahedral mesh was used. Inflation layers were added to the air duct walls so that the boundary layer within the airflow could be captured.

A mesh independence study was conducted to make sure that the results of the numerical model were independent of the size of the developed mesh. For this study, four meshes were developed. Each subsequent mesh had an increment of approximately 50% elements. The velocity profile at the middle of the air duct was obtained and compared, as shown in Fig. 6 (a). An average velocity was also calculated and plotted against the number of elements, as shown in Fig. 6(b). Based on the results, it can be seen that there is no significant difference beyond the mesh size of 614,024 elements; therefore, the medium mesh was selected.

Furthermore, a time independence study was performed to ensure that the results of the numerical model are independent of the size of the time-step. This is required to reduce the computation power while maintaining the accuracy of the results. The result of this study is shown in Fig. 6(c). Based on the results, the time step size of 0.5 s was selected.

In this study transient numerical simulations were performed. All the simulations were conducted at atmospheric pressure with 25 °C ambient temperature. The inlet velocity of airflow for Strategy 1 was set to 1.6 m/s, which was obtained through Experiment 1. For Strategy 2 and 3, it was set to 2.17 m/s. The Reynold number was kept the same for all the strategies at 1950 and was based on the hydraulic diameter of the air duct. Since the cross-section area in Strategy 2 and 3 was reduced due to the cold plate protrusion, therefore, the velocity of airflow was increased to maintain the same Reynolds number. For validation purposes an identical replica was created based on Experiment 1, and the material properties were used based on Table 1. Once the model was validated, then, for all the strategies, an all-aluminum housing was used rather than wood, and the PCM was changed to paraffin with a lower phase change temperature to increase cooling and temperature uniformity. The properties of the materials used are shown in Table 2. Finally, the heat flux boundary condition was used at the surface of the cells, which was obtained through Experiment 2.

The numerical simulations provide effective results; however, it is important to validate the results of the numerical model with the experimental results. Since the numerical model is based on the battery module developed for Experiment 1, therefore, the results of Experiment 1 were used for validation. The validation results are shown in Fig. 7 . It can be seen that the numerical results follow the experimental results closely and stay within the experimental error of ± 2%. Therefore, the results of the numerical model agree with the experimental results and the model is considered to be completely validated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Heat flux profile at different discharge rates
ce:section.ce:para: Experiment 2 was used to obtain the heat flux profiles of the 18,650 Li-ion cell. The cell was discharged at 1 C (2.5 A), 2 C (5 A), 3 C (7.5 A), 5 C (12.5 A), and 7 C (17.5 A) discharge rates. The results of the experiment are provided in Fig. 8 . The maximum heat flux obtained at the end of the discharge cycle at 1 C is 170 W/m2; at 2 C, it is 323 W/m2; at 3 C, it is 382 W/m2; at 5 C, it is 540 W/m2; and at 7 C, it is 584 W/m2. In comparison, the highest heat flux throughout the discharge time was obtained for 7 C discharge rate; hence, 7 C was considered as the worst-case scenario and the numerical simulations for all the strategies were conducted at this discharge rate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Overview of thermal profiles of the proposed strategies
ce:section.ce:para: The developed and validated numerical model was used to conduct numerical simulations. The optimal operating temperature range for the Li-ion cells is from 20 – 40 °C and a tolerable range for the operation ranges from 40 – 50 °C. Additionally, the temperature difference between the maximum and the minimum temperature (also referred to as temperature uniformity) should be maintained within 5 °C, which is essential for the proper regulation of the electrical balance within the battery pack and to prolong the life of the cells. The maximum and minimum temperatures for all the strategies are shown in Fig. 9 .

For Strategy 1 it can be seen from Fig. 9(a) that the maximum temperature increase reaches 30 °C, which is the phase change temperature of the PCM (from Table 3 ) by 405 s. At this point, the phase change of the PCM starts, and the maximum temperature increases to 30.5 °C by the end of the discharge rate at 514 s. Any heat generated beyond 405 s is extracted through the latent heat of the PCM, which maintains the maximum temperature at ∼30 °C. For Strategy 2, the maximum curve is similar to Strategy 1, as shown in Fig. 9(b). In this as well, the maximum temperature reaches 30 °C by 405 s. For both these strategies, in terms of percentage, the maximum temperature stays at ∼30 °C for 21.2% of the total discharge time. Additionally, it can be seen from Fig. 9(c) for Strategy 3 that the maximum temperature increase throughout the discharge cycle is up to 29.1 °C by the end of discharge. The phase change of the PCM starts at 30 °C, and in this case, no phase change occurs since the temperature does not reach the phase change temperature of the PCM. The longer it takes for the maximum temperature to reach the phase change temperature and the least it stays at this temperature until the completion of discharge is beneficial for the TMS as this will result in the lowest utilization of the PCM.

It can be seen from Fig. 9 that in all strategies, the minimum temperature of the battery module does not reach the phase change temperature. It increases throughout the discharge, and at the end of discharge, it reaches 27.7 °C. The maximum temperature increases at a higher rate compared to the increase in the minimum temperature. This difference in temperature increase rates results in a temperature non-uniformity in the battery module and is shown in Fig. 10 .

It can be seen from Fig. 10 that the temperature uniformity (difference between the maximum and minimum temperature) generally reduces with increasing discharge time. In Strategies 1 and 2, the rate of maximum temperature compared to the rate of minimum temperature increases; therefore, the temperature uniformity reduces at a higher rate until the phase change temperature of the PCM is reached, and then it starts to increase until the end of the discharge cycle. Once the phase change temperature is reached, the maximum temperature does not increase, whereas the minimum temperature keeps increasing. This reduces the temperature difference, which then increases the temperature uniformity. For Strategy 3, due to effective cooling from the cold plates, the rate of maximum temperature increase is significantly lower; therefore, the decrease in temperature uniformity throughout the discharge cycle is significantly lower.

In order to better understand the cooling phenomenon, the temperature contours of all the cells at the end of the discharge cycle for the three strategies are shown in Fig. 11 . In all the strategies, it can be seen that the surfaces of the cells that are in direct contact with the top and bottom plate of the battery module have relatively lower temperatures due to heat conduction from the cell to the module housing. Compared to the surface in contact with the bottom plate, the surface of the cell in contact with the top plate exhibits the minimum temperatures due to the air-cooling effect that occurs at the top plate. Therefore, the top plate cools due to air cooling, and then this allows additional conduction from the cells’ surface to the top plate. For Strategies 1 and 2, it can be seen that the high temperature locations are at the center of the cells, specifically the surfaces facing the neighboring cells. Whereas the surfaces facing the housing of the battery module have lower temperatures. For Strategy 2, even lower temperature surfaces are the surfaces that are directly facing the cold plates. This shows the effectiveness of the cold plate utilization as it extracts a higher amount of heat from the PCM material, which increases the heat extraction capacity of the PCM, resulting in increased cooling from the surface of the cells. Finally, for Strategy 3, due to the presence of cold plates in between all the surfaces of the neighboring cells, the highest amount of cooling is achieved compared to the other two strategies. The lower temperature surfaces are the ones that are in front of the cold plates, whereas the higher temperature surfaces are the ones that are facing the module housing. Since the module housing is cooled through the ambient temperature and the airflow at the top of the battery module only as compared to the liquid cooling effect for the other cell surfaces therefore, these temperatures are higher in comparison. This shows the effectiveness and importance of the presence of liquid cooling within the proposed battery module.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of secondary liquid and air cooling
ce:section.ce:para: In order to better visualize the effect of liquid cooling, the temperature contours at the center of the battery module towards the end of the discharge cycle are provided in Fig. 12 . In all the strategies, the highest temperature of the PCM is exhibited at the contact surface with the cells. For Strategy 1 and 2, the PCM that is in between the surfaces of the two neighboring cells has higher temperatures compared to the rest of the PCM. In Strategy 1, the fluid in the liquid channel acts as a heat sink for the PCM, which reduces the temperature of the PCM around the liquid channels. Additionally, the battery module also acts as a heat sink for the PCM, and the PCM near the housing of the battery module has the lowest temperatures. The housing is cooled by the ambient temperature and the airflow within the air duct. Moreover, the cold plates also act as a heat sink for the PCM, and the effect of cold plates can be seen in Strategies 2 and 3. In Strategy 2, through the addition of a cold plate, the high temperature region with the PCM in the transverse direction is eliminated as all the fluid extracts the heat from the PCM. Similarly, through the placement of additional cold plates in the longitudinal direction, the high temperature regions between all the neighboring cells have been eliminated in Strategy 3. In Strategies 1 and 2, the high temperature regions in between the cells are the cause of the high temperature increase rate and increased temperature uniformity, as shown in Fig. 9(a), (b), and Fig. 10. As a result, the melting of the PCM starts in these regions. However, in Strategy 3 these high temperature regions are eliminated; therefore, the temperature does not reach the phase change temperature of the PCM, and the melting of the PCM does not occur.

The temperature contours for the liquid channels and cold plates are provided in Fig. 13 . It can be seen that in Strategy 1, due to the minimal contact surface between the liquid channel and the PCM, the heat extraction from the PCM is lowest. Whereas, in Strategy 2, there is an increased heat extraction through the increased contact surface area. Finally, the highest heat extraction is achieved in Strategy 3 as it has the highest contact surface area with the PCM. Moreover, for Strategies 2 and 3, the lower portion of the fluid has the highest temperature, and it gradually reduces to the minimum towards the top portion of the fluid. This is due to the air-cooling effect that occurs at the top of the cold plates that is protruded within the air duct. Additionally, the lowest temperature of the fluid is exhibited near the inlet of the air duct and as the air flows through the cold plate, its heat extraction capacity reduces, and the temperature of the fluid increases towards the outlet of the air duct. The impact of the single fluid body in Strategy 3 can be seen in Fig. 13(c), as the same airflow allows the fluid to cool in between the cell columns as well.

Since the airflow has a low velocity and is considered laminar flow with a Reynold number of 1950 therefore, the temperature of airflow near the walls of the battery module and the cold plates will be the highest as compared to airflow in the center of the air duct. Hence, the temperature contours of airflow at the walls are shown in Fig. 14 . In all the strategies, the temperature of airflow near the inlet is the lowest, and it increases as it extracts heat and moves toward the outlet of the battery module. It can be seen that the lowest heat transfer to airflow occurs in Strategy 1, and the heat transfer increases slightly by 1.1% as it flows over the liquid channels. Whereas, in Strategy 2, the heat transfer is high as it flows over the cold plates because of the increased contact surface area with the protruding cold plates in the air duct. Finally, in Strategy 3, there is increased heat accumulation in the cold plates resulting in higher heat extraction from the airflow and the highest cooling achieved.

In the battery module, the PCM is stationary and constricted in the module housing. Once the latent heat of the PCM is completely used and all the PCM has melted, it will start to act as a thermal insulator, and further heat extraction from the cell will not occur, which can lead to safety issues for the battery module. Therefore, it is crucial to extract heat away from the PCM. As discussed previously, the secondary cooling through liquid and air has assisted in removing heat from the PCM, resulting in an increase in its heat extraction capacity. However, it is necessary to study the amount of PCM that has melted in the discharge process. This is obtained from the liquid fraction term in the solidification/melting model described in Section 2.3.1. In Strategy 1, due to low heat extraction from the circular liquid channels, the highest amount of PCM melting occurred at 0.12%. This is followed by Strategy 2 with a PCM melting of 0.07%, and finally, due to exceptional cooling from the secondary coolants, none of the PCM was melted in Strategy 3. Overall, the melting amount of PCM is very low, even at a high discharge rate of 7 C. This shows that due to the secondary cooling, the amount of PCM that changes phase is low, and it is capable of extracting additional heat energy from the cells.

In this study, phase change material (PCM) based hybrid thermal management strategies are proposed for cylindrical Lithium-ion battery modules. Experimental and numerical studies are conducted to analyze the proposed strategies at a high discharge rate of 7 C. Three different types of thermal management configurations are developed by utilizing a single primary coolant and dual secondary coolants. The primary coolant is the PCM, and the secondary coolants are liquid and air. The experimental studies were conducted to obtain the temperature and heat flux profiles of the battery module. Moreover, a numerical model is developed and validated using the experimental data obtained. Using the numerical model, the thermal performance of the battery module was obtained for the proposed strategies. The results show that the highest cooling was achieved through Strategy 3 as the maximum temperature was limited to 29.1 °C and a high temperature uniformity was achieved by the end of the discharge cycle with the difference between the maximum and minimum temperature limited to 1.2 °C. Additionally, none of the PCM was melted as the maximum temperature was maintained below the phase change temperature of the PCM. This shows that due to the secondary cooling, the PCM has increased heat extraction capacity and can extract additional heat from the cells. Therefore, it can be concluded that Strategy 3 is capable of maintaining the required optimal thermal operating conditions without changing the phase of the PCM. Furthermore, it eliminates the requirement of a pump and reservoir since there is no flow of liquid within the battery module. This reduces the energy required for the operation of the thermal management system, thereby increasing the available energy for propulsion.

As concluded above, the hybrid thermal management strategy is capable of maintaining the battery module temperatures within the feasible operating range while significantly reducing the energy requirements for the operation of the thermal management system. However, gaps still remain which require further investigation and research in order to apply this strategy to practical applications. The limitations of the research performed in this thesis are as follows:
•
The research was conducted on 18,650 Li-ion cells and it is not applicable to larger cells without further investigation.
•
The research was conducted on cylindrical Li-ion cells and it is not applicable to different cell geometries without further investigation.
•
The research is not applicable to discharge rates higher than 7 C without further investigation.
•
The structural integrity of various developed components has not been investigated.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: Fast Facts on Transportation Greenhouse Gas Emissions | US EPA n.d. https://www.epa.gov/greenvehicles/fast-facts-transportation-greenhouse-gas-emissions (accessed September 5, 2022).

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Seham Shahid is a PhD candidate in the Faculty of Engineering and Applied Science at Ontario Tech University, Oshawa, Canada. He completed his BEng degree in Mechanical Engineering from University of Nottingham (2014) and MASc degree in Mechanical Engineering from Ontario Tech University (2017). His-research interests are in the area of thermofluids and energy systems.

Dr. Martin Agelin-Chaab is a professor of Mechanical Engineering at Ontario Tech University. He previously served as the Graduate Program Director and currently the Department Chair. His-research interests are in the area of thermofluids and energy systems, where he has made significant contributions, including over 200 peer-reviewed publications and 4 book chapters. He has also previously served as the Chair of the Fluid Mechanics Engineering Technical Committee of the CSME.
